intro.overview: Culmination of the two-week LoneStar DevOps assessment. Amanda Nguyen presents to Longhorn Ventures in one week; needs an investor-ready report. Sarah Martinez needs a technical roadmap her team can actually implement. You have everything you need from Lab 5.1 — this week is about assembling it into a professional deliverable that serves both audiences.

mentorQuote.title: Founder, NEXUS Security
mentorQuote.quote: The executive summary is the most important page in the report. Amanda will hand it to investors who won't read the rest. One page, clear language, no jargon. If you can't explain the risk in plain English, you don't understand it well enough yet.

focusAreas: Executive summary writing for non-technical audiences
Business constraint integration in security recommendations
objectives: Compile vulnerability findings and risk analysis from Lab 5.1 into a cohesive report
Write an executive summary suitable for investor presentation
Develop a prioritized remediation roadmap aligned to LoneStar’s 4-week constraint
Provide actionable recommendations Sarah’s team can implement

callouts.title: .docx Format Required
callouts.body: PDF submissions receive an automatic zero. If you drafted in Google Docs, export to .docx before submitting.

callouts.title: Minimum Requirements
callouts.body: 10+ references required. All screenshots from Lab 5.1 must be included. VM name must be visible in all terminal screenshots (kali-lastname-studentID). Use naming convention: ##-description.png.

youAlreadyHave.subtitle: Two-Week Engagement Model
youAlreadyHave.intro: This is NOT starting from scratch. You’ve been building this report all week in Lab 5.1.

sections.title: From Lab 5.1 — Vulnerability Assessment & Risk Analysis
items: Environment specifications (LoneStar DevOps target systems and Docker architecture)
Nmap scanning methodology and combined -sV -sC results
WPScan findings from the WordPress portal
MySQL and SSH credential testing results
Top 10 vulnerabilities selected and documented with rationale
CVSS 3.1 scores and severity ratings for all 10 findings
5×5 risk matrix with findings plotted and color coded
Prioritized remediation roadmap draft
10 screenshots documenting your full methodology

sections.title: NEW This Week
items: Title Page, Table of Contents, Executive Summary
NEXUS Timesheet (Section A)
Finalized Remediation Roadmap with phased timelines
Long-term Security Recommendations
Professional Reflection, References, Appendix

callouts.title: You’re Already 50–60% Done
callouts.body: Your vulnerability inventory, CVSS table, risk matrix, and roadmap draft are done. This week is compilation, executive summary, and professional polish — not starting over.

timeGuide.timesheetNote: Time estimates assume you completed Lab 5.1 thoroughly. If your earlier documentation is sparse, budget additional time.
timeGuide.introText: This lab focuses on report compilation and new analysis. Most content comes from Lab 5.1.

content.whatYoureDoing: Billing justification — document all hours across the 2-week assessment.
content.whyItMatters: Consulting engagements require time accountability. Clients and firm management need to verify that billed hours match actual work performed.

content.title: Include in Section A
items: Week-by-week hour breakdown (Lab 5.1 and Lab 5.2)
Internal vs. client-billable hour split
Total hours across both weeks
Justification narrative for each line item

content.title: Textbook Reference
content.text: Chapter 10 covers infrastructure security and the consulting engagement lifecycle. Your Section A timesheet documents exactly the billable activity pattern described in Chapter 10’s coverage of security assessment engagements.

items.label: Generic descriptions
items.text: “Did lab” is not a billable line item. Use specific activity names (e.g., “Nmap service enumeration and NSE vulnerability scanning”)

items.label: Unrealistic time entries
items.text: Entries that don’t reflect actual work raise flags in a real consulting environment

items.label: Missing internal training hours
items.text: Your Lab 5.1 scanning and analysis work counts as internal training hours — those belong on the timesheet

content.text: A complete timesheet showing 7–10 hours distributed across both weeks, with specific justifications for each entry.

content.whatYoureDoing: Document the assessment methodology so another NEXUS analyst could reproduce the entire engagement.
content.whyItMatters: NEXUS builds institutional knowledge through runbooks. If you leave the firm tomorrow, another analyst needs to be able to repeat your work for follow-up engagements.

content.title: Include in Section B
items: Environment specifications and target system details (Docker architecture, container IPs, services)
Scanning commands with exact parameters used (nmap flags, WPScan options)
CVSS scoring methodology and selection rationale for your top 10
Risk matrix framework and scoring criteria
Lessons learned and recommendations for future assessments

content.title: Textbook Reference
content.text: Chapter 10 discusses vulnerability assessment methodology and how analysts document reproducible procedures. Your runbook is precisely this kind of institutional knowledge transfer. Reference Chapter 10’s sections on assessment documentation standards when describing your methodology.

items.label: Copy-pasting lab instructions
items.text: Document what you actually did, not what the lab told you to do. There’s a difference

items.label: Missing context
items.text: Commands without explanation of why you ran them aren’t reproducible documentation

items.label: No screenshots
items.text: Your Lab 5.1 screenshots belong in the runbook as evidence that the steps produce the expected output

content.text: A reproducible runbook another NEXUS analyst could follow to repeat the LoneStar DevOps vulnerability assessment from scratch.

content.whatYoureDoing: Write a one-page summary Amanda can hand directly to Longhorn Ventures investors — no jargon, no technical detail.
content.whyItMatters: Investors don’t read full security reports. Amanda’s credibility with Longhorn Ventures depends entirely on this single page.

content.title: Include in Executive Summary
items: Assessment scope and timeline
Key findings summary (plain language — no CVE numbers)
Business risk statement (what this means for LoneStar’s operations and Series B)
High-level remediation plan overview
Clear recommendation and proposed next steps

content.body: NIST SP 800-30 — Guide for Conducting Risk Assessments. Cite this when describing your risk communication approach.

content.title: Textbook Reference
content.text: Chapter 20 covers risk management reporting and stakeholder communication — the same non-technical framing your executive summary requires. NIST SP 800-30 provides the risk communication framework that informed your executive summary structure.

items.label: Too technical
items.text: If you mention CVSS or CVE IDs, rewrite the sentence in plain English

items.label: Too long
items.text: One page maximum, no exceptions

items.label: No clear recommendation
items.text: Executives need to know what to do next. End with a specific action

content.text: A one-page executive summary a non-technical CEO can read and understand in three minutes.

content.whatYoureDoing: Compile your top 10 vulnerability findings and risk analysis from Lab 5.1 into client report format.
content.whyItMatters: Sarah needs technical details to fix the vulnerabilities. This section tells LoneStar exactly what was found, how it was scored, and why it matters to their business.

content.title: Lead DevOps Engineer, LoneStar DevOps
content.quote: Give me actionable steps. Tell me the order, the testing required, the downtime implications. I can’t fix what I don’t understand.

content.title: Include in Technical Findings
items: Your top 10 vulnerabilities with selection rationale
CVSS 3.1 scores and severity ratings for each finding
Business impact statement for each critical/high finding
5×5 risk matrix with findings plotted
Evidence screenshots from Lab 5.1
CVE reference numbers where applicable

content.body: What is the difference between a vulnerability assessment and a penetration test? Your report is the former — understanding the distinction matters for setting client expectations.

items: PTES — Penetration Testing Execution Standard: http://www.pentest-standard.org/
NIST SP 800-115 — Technical Guide to Information Security Testing and Assessment

content.body: Chapter 10 covers infrastructure security assessment methodology — how analysts document devices, services, and vulnerabilities in a structured, reproducible format. Chapter 20’s Business Risks and Qualitatively Assessing Risk sections provide the framework for your CVSS scores and risk matrix. Both should appear in your reference list.

items.label: Raw scanner output pasted directly
items.text: Format findings for a client audience — terminal dumps belong in the appendix, not the findings section

items.label: Missing evidence
items.text: Every finding needs a supporting screenshot from Lab 5.1

items.label: Inconsistent severity ratings
items.text: Your written severity labels must match your CVSS scores

content.text: A complete technical findings section with your top 10 vulnerabilities, CVSS scores, business impact statements, and risk matrix visualization.

content.whatYoureDoing: Expand the remediation roadmap draft from Lab 5.1 into a complete phased plan with timelines, specific actions, and realistic recommendations.
content.whyItMatters: Sarah needs a realistic plan she can actually execute with two engineers and a limited budget in four weeks. A list of fixes is not a roadmap.

content.title: Include in Remediation Roadmap
items: Phase 1 (Week 1) — Quick wins and critical vulnerabilities (disable jdavis, change default credentials)
Phase 2 (Weeks 2–3) — High-priority remediations
Phase 3 (Week 4+) — Medium-priority and hardening measures
Specific remediation actions for each finding
Testing and validation steps after each remediation
Downtime and maintenance window considerations

content.body: What is a vulnerability management program? Remediation isn’t a one-time event — it’s a continuous cycle of discover, assess, prioritize, remediate, and verify.
NIST SP 800-40 Rev. 4 — Guide to Enterprise Patch Management Planning. Directly applicable to LoneStar’s remediation strategy.
Chapter 20 — Risk Mitigation Strategies covers change management, change control processes, and rollback procedures. These apply directly to your remediation phases.

items.label: No phases
items.text: A flat list of fixes is not a roadmap. Sequence matters

items.label: Ignoring constraints
items.text: 2 engineers, limited budget, 4-week window, zero-downtime requirement. Your roadmap must work within these

items.label: Missing quick wins
items.text: Phase 1 should show early progress. Disabling the jdavis account takes five minutes and eliminates a critical risk

content.text: A phased remediation roadmap with realistic timelines that respects LoneStar’s resource and time constraints.

content.whatYoureDoing: Complete a final quality review of the entire report before submission.
content.whyItMatters: Typos and formatting errors undermine credibility. Longhorn Ventures is evaluating LoneStar — and by extension, NEXUS. Your report reflects on both.

content.title: Final Assembly Checklist
items: Section A: Timesheet complete with hour justifications for both weeks
Section B: Runbook is reproducible by another analyst
Section C: Executive summary fits on one page
Section C: Top 10 vulnerabilities documented with CVSS scores
Section C: Risk matrix included and color coded
Section C: Remediation roadmap with phased timelines
All Lab 5.1 screenshots included as evidence
VM name visible in terminal screenshots (kali-lastname-studentID)
Minimum 10 references included
Document is .docx format (not PDF)
Professional formatting and grammar throughout

items.label: Inconsistent formatting
items.text: Sections compiled from different sources often have mismatched fonts, heading sizes, and spacing — do a final formatting pass

items.label: Missing page numbers
items.text: A multi-section professional report needs page numbers

items.label: Weak references
items.text: “Google.com” is not a valid citation. Every reference needs author, title, organization, URL, and access date

content.text: A complete, polished Engagement Packet ready for submission: all sections finalized, updated Table of Contents with page numbers, all Lab 5.1 screenshots embedded with captions, VM name visible in every terminal screenshot, 10+ valid references listed, professional formatting and grammar reviewed, and the file saved as IS3513_Lab52_Lastname_Firstname.docx.

Chapters 10 and 20 together span the full arc of this module: Chapter 10 covers infrastructure security and vulnerability assessment methodology; Chapter 20 covers risk management, business impact analysis, and remediation planning. Both should appear in your final reference list.

content.body: Upload your completed Engagement Packet (.docx) to Lab 5.2 in Canvas. This assignment is manually graded. Expect grades within one week of the due date.

items.text: Vulnerability discovery informs attack planning. Understanding how defenders prioritize helps attackers identify overlooked vectors. The jdavis account is the kind of finding real pentesters love — technical teams often miss human factors.

Risk assessment and remediation planning are core defensive skills. Your report serves two audiences — executives who approve budgets and engineers who implement fixes. Both need to leave with a clear picture of what happens next.

purpleTeam.connection: Both perspectives inform the complete risk picture. Discovery serves both attack planning AND asset inventory. Risk assessment considers exploitability AND business impact. The roadmap addresses offensive findings AND defensive priorities. Your report should help Amanda (business view) AND Sarah (technical view) — that’s purple team thinking in action.

sections.title: Report Sections
items: Title Page
Table of Contents
Executive Summary
Section A — NEXUS Timesheet
Section B — Internal Runbook
Section C — Client Report
Professional Reflection
References (minimum 10)
Appendix

sections.title: Documentation Verification
items: All Lab 5.1 screenshots embedded as evidence
VM name visible in all terminal screenshots
Captions present on all screenshots
10+ valid references included

sections.title: Submission Format
items: .docx format (not PDF)
Filename: IS3513_Lab52_Lastname_Firstname.docx
Professional formatting throughout
Grammar and spelling checked

gradingStandards.automaticZero: No submission, AI-generated content, fabricated screenshots or tool outputs, work shared with or from another student, wrong file format (PDF instead of .docx), or academic integrity violation.

needHelp.lateWorkPolicy: Late work is not accepted. Drop policies exist for emergencies. This Engagement Packet is eligible to be your one dropped score.

permitted: Proofread and grammar check your writing
Explain technical concepts to verify your accuracy
prohibited: Create fake screenshots or tool outputs
genAI.principle: AI is a tutor, not a ghostwriter.

whatsNext.title: Module 5 Complete!
whatsNext.body: You’ve completed the full security assessment lifecycle — from vulnerability discovery through risk analysis to professional reporting. These are the same skills NEXUS uses with real clients. Complete the Module 5 Exam covering Chapters 10 and 20 to finish the module.
